channel.title: Jax Morning Brief
channel.description: Your daily rundown of national headlines, Jacksonville and Northeast Florida local news, home lending updates, and the latest in AI. Produced by Akilesh.
channel.itunes:author: Akilesh

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — S and P Erases Iran War Losses, Anthropic Hits 30B ARR, Winn-Dixie Stays
item.description: Markets close at their highest since the Iran war began as ceasefire talk builds. Anthropic's revenue doubles in two months. And Jacksonville weighs a 12 million dollar deal to keep Winn-Dixie at home.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Tuesday, April 14th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief. I'm Jenny.

ANDREW: And I'm Andrew.

JENNY: Wall Street just erased every point it lost when the United States and Israel went to war with Iran in February.

ANDREW: And in the AI race, Anthropic's annual revenue run rate has more than doubled in eight weeks, vaulting past thirty billion dollars.

JENNY: Let's get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed at 6,886, up about one percent. The Dow added 302 points to finish at 48,218, a gain of roughly six tenths of a percent. And the Nasdaq led the pack, up one point two percent to 23,184. That was the highest close for the S and P since before the Iran war began on February 28th, a remarkable round trip given that oil markets remain disrupted and the Strait of Hormuz is still effectively closed. The ten-year Treasury yield is sitting just above 4.35 percent this morning, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking around 6.27 percent according to Mortgage News Daily. Rates have drifted down from their March highs as traders price in a more cautious Fed and softer jobs data.

ANDREW: Let's start with what's driving all of this. The rally you're seeing on Wall Street is not a vote of confidence that the Middle East has calmed down. It's a bet that the worst-case scenario is off the table.

JENNY: Unpack that for us. What exactly are traders pricing in?

ANDREW: Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has been blocked by Iran since late February, after the U.S. and Israeli air campaign and the killing of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. About a quarter of the world's seaborne oil and a fifth of its liquefied natural gas normally flow through that strait. What's changed in the last forty-eight hours is that back-channel diplomacy through Oman and Qatar appears to be gaining traction, with reports that Tehran is exploring a partial reopening in exchange for a freeze on further strikes. Traders are positioned for a ceasefire.

JENNY: And if the talks collapse?

ANDREW: The mirror image of this week's rally is exactly what you'd expect.

ANDREW: Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve is heading into one of the most consequential leadership transitions in its modern history. Jay Powell's term as chair ends May 15th, just thirty-one days from now, and the White House has not yet named a successor. Reporting from the Wall Street Journal and Reuters suggests the shortlist has narrowed to three names, all of whom are viewed as more dovish than Powell on rates. The bond market is already leaning into that read. A Powell replacement seen as friendlier to rate cuts is a big part of why the ten-year yield has softened even as oil prices remain elevated. Today, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee speaks at the Semafor World Economy conference in Washington. He'll be one of the most closely watched voices at the Fed over the next few weeks, and any hint about the July meeting will move markets.

ANDREW: And on the trade front, the administration's pharmaceutical tariff proclamation, signed April 2nd, is starting to hit earnings guidance. The tariffs take effect July 31st for named companies and September 29th for everyone else. Eli Lilly, Merck, and Pfizer all flagged it on investor calls last week. The immediate question for consumers is whether generic drug prices spike ahead of the deadline as importers try to front-run the duties. Early wholesale data from Bloomberg suggests they already are.

JENNY: Andrew, speaking of rates — what's happening on the mortgage side?

ANDREW: Rates are quietly doing something interesting. The thirty-year fixed is back near 6.27 percent this morning, down from the 6.55 range we were seeing in mid-March. For a buyer taking out a 400,000 dollar mortgage, that roughly thirty basis point move is worth about seventy-five dollars a month in lower payments. It's not transformative, but it's real.

JENNY: So are buyers jumping back in?

ANDREW: That's the catch — they aren't biting. The Mortgage Bankers Association reported applications fell eight tenths of a percent for the week ending April 3rd, even as rates eased. Purchase applications ticked up one percent week over week but are still running seven percent below last year. Refinance demand is sitting at its lowest level since December. The signal here is that after two years of waiting for rates to break, a lot of would-be buyers have just stopped watching.

ANDREW: One bright spot worth flagging. FHA purchase applications jumped five percent on the week, and the FHA thirty-year fixed is now averaging 6.22 percent. For first-time buyers and lower-down-payment borrowers, that gap below the conventional rate is unusually wide. HousingWire is reporting that several large non-bank lenders are leaning hard into FHA product to pick up volume the conventional channel can't find. If that trend holds, it's going to reshape the mix of who's originating what through the spring buying season.

ANDREW: On the regulatory front, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau remains in flux under its acting director, and the Federal Housing Finance Agency has yet to finalize its revised capital rules for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. Both agencies were expected to issue guidance this month on artificial intelligence use in underwriting. That guidance has now slipped to May at the earliest, according to two sources familiar with the matter cited by National Mortgage News. For lenders who have been holding back automated decisioning investments waiting for clarity, that's another month of limbo.

ANDREW: Jenny, over to you — AI had a massive week.

JENNY: It really did. The story of the week is Anthropic. The company's annual recurring revenue has climbed from fourteen billion dollars in February to more than thirty billion dollars this week, according to reporting from The Next Platform and confirmed by Anthropic leadership.

ANDREW: Put that in perspective for us.

JENNY: That's a faster revenue ramp than any enterprise software company in history, and it has now pulled essentially even with OpenAI, whose ARR drifted down from twenty-five billion to twenty-four billion over the same period. Anthropic also said it has more than doubled its base of customers paying one million dollars or more annually for Claude, from around five hundred in February to over a thousand today. A lot of that growth is coming from financial services and legal, two sectors where agentic workflows are finally producing measurable returns.

JENNY: On a related note, Broadcom announced an expanded chip deal with Anthropic that will give the company access to roughly three point five gigawatts of compute capacity running on Google's custom AI processors. Three point five gigawatts is, for context, the rough power draw of the entire city of Miami. This is the kind of infrastructure footprint that only a handful of companies in the world can even attempt, and it's happening alongside similar buildouts at OpenAI and Google. The AI capex supercycle is not slowing down.

JENNY: And in a development that caught a lot of attention, OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google confirmed last week that they are now sharing threat intelligence through the Frontier Model Forum specifically to counter what they describe as systematic model theft by Chinese AI firms. Anthropic alone says it has documented sixteen million unauthorized exchanges from three named companies — DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax — that appear designed to distill Claude's capabilities into cheaper Chinese models.

ANDREW: That's the first time the three leading American labs have openly coordinated on a security issue.

JENNY: Right, and it likely previews an export-control push later this year. If you're an enterprise buyer evaluating Chinese open-weights models, the provenance question just got a lot sharper.

JENNY: Closing out here in Jacksonville. Weather-wise, the city is looking at a high near 80 degrees and mostly sunny skies today, with a comfortable low in the low sixties overnight. It's a near-perfect spring day for the first republic.

JENNY: The biggest local story continues to be the JEA investigation. The City Council's special investigative committee met Monday for the third time, and JEA confirmed it received a subpoena Friday from State Attorney Melissa Nelson's office seeking a broad sweep of communication records tied to CEO Vickie Cavey, Council President Kevin Carrico, and a lobbying firm that employs former Mayor Lenny Curry.

ANDREW: That escalates things significantly.

JENNY: It does. The subpoena turns what had been a slow-moving political fight into a formal criminal inquiry. If you lived through the 2019 JEA privatization debacle, the playbook here is going to feel very familiar — and this one is unfolding while the utility is trying to negotiate a major new natural gas contract.

JENNY: On the economic development side, the city is weighing up to twelve million dollars in incentives to keep Winn-Dixie's headquarters in Jacksonville and add two hundred full-time jobs. Winn-Dixie, now owned by Aldi, has been quietly exploring relocation options, and losing its headquarters would be a significant blow to the downtown office market, which is already dealing with elevated vacancy. The deal is expected to go before City Council within the next two weeks.

JENNY: And a quieter but important story: the University of Florida has selected Edgemoor Infrastructure and Real Estate, based in Maryland, to lead the first phase of its new graduate campus downtown. The initial build-out is valued at 287 million dollars and will anchor the south bank redevelopment. The UF campus has been sold for years as the single biggest catalyst for downtown Jacksonville's long-term economic trajectory, and picking a developer moves it from press release to construction timeline. Expect groundbreaking talk by late summer.

JENNY: One more thing on the local front worth flagging. JTA ridership on fixed bus routes fell roughly eighteen percent in February and ten percent in March compared to last year, even as the agency is two months into a fare-reduction pilot that was supposed to boost ridership. That's a counterintuitive result, and it raises real questions about whether the problem is price or network design. JTA leadership is expected to address the numbers at its board meeting later this month.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this week: the March CPI release from the Bureau of Labor Statistics, which drops Thursday morning at 8:30 a.m. Eastern. Economists surveyed by Bloomberg are expecting headline inflation to come in at around three point one percent year over year, with core at three point four percent. The critical number inside the report is energy — specifically how much of the post-war oil spike is bleeding into the data. A hot print resets the Fed succession debate and puts the mortgage rate rally at risk. A cool print gives the doves a lot more room. Either way, it's the most important data release of the month.

JENNY: That's your Morning Brief for Tuesday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We'll see you tomorrow.
